 {"id":"bd-4d8m","title":"Implement Tower feature flag and optional dependency","description":"## Overview

Make Tower integration optional via feature flag to avoid mandatory dependencies.

## Requirements

### Feature Flag
```toml
# Cargo.toml
[features]
default = []
tower = ["dep:tower", "dep:tower-service"]

[dependencies]
tower = { version = "0.4", optional = true }
tower-service = { version = "0.3", optional = true }
```

### Conditional Compilation
```rust
// src/tower/mod.rs
#![cfg(feature = "tower")]

mod adapter;
mod service;

pub use adapter::{AsupersyncAdapter, TowerAdapter};
pub use service::AsupersyncService;
```

### Re-exports
```rust
// src/lib.rs
#[cfg(feature = "tower")]
pub mod tower;
```

### Documentation
Document feature in:
- README.md
- lib.rs module docs
- Cargo.toml metadata

## Acceptance Criteria
1. "tower" feature flag
2. Compiles without tower feature
3. tower module only present with feature
4. Feature documented in README
5. CI tests both with and without feature

## Test Requirements
- Test compilation without feature
- Test compilation with feature
- Test feature detection at runtime","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CopperCreek","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:04:12.038825557Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-22T15:50:20.347675020Z","closed_at":"2026-01-22T15:50:20.347613194Z","close_reason":"fixed","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-4k7","title":"Implement test oracle: losers_always_drained invariant checker","description":"## Purpose
Implement a test oracle that verifies the "losers always drained" invariant: in any race/select operation, losing tasks are cancelled AND fully drained before the operation returns.

## The Invariant
From asupersync_plan_v4.md:
> Races must cancel and fully drain losers

This prevents resource leaks, ensures cleanup runs, and maintains predictable behavior.

## What "Drained" Means
A task is drained when it has:
1. Received cancel request (`CancelRequested` state)
2. Run to next checkpoint (`Cancelling` state)
3. Executed finalizers (`Finalizing` state)
4. Reached `Completed(Cancelled)` state

Simply requesting cancellation is NOT sufficient. The task must actually complete.

## Oracle Design

```rust
pub struct LoserDrainOracle {
    // Tracks race/select operations
    race_starts: Vec<RaceEvent>,
    race_completes: Vec<RaceCompletion>,
    task_drains: HashMap<TaskId, DrainRecord>,
}

pub struct RaceEvent {
    pub race_id: RaceId,
    pub participants: Vec<TaskId>,
    pub start_time: Time,
}

pub struct RaceCompletion {
    pub race_id: RaceId,
    pub winner: TaskId,
    pub losers: Vec<TaskId>,
    pub complete_time: Time,
}

pub struct DrainRecord {
    pub cancel_requested: Option<Time>,
    pub drain_started: Option<Time>,
    pub finalizers_complete: Option<Time>,
    pub fully_drained: Option<Time>,
}

impl LoserDrainOracle {
    /// Called when race/select starts
    pub fn on_race_start(&mut self, race_id: RaceId, participants: Vec<TaskId>, time: Time);
    
    /// Called when race determines winner
    pub fn on_race_complete(&mut self, race_id: RaceId, winner: TaskId, time: Time);
    
    /// Called when task fully drains
    pub fn on_task_drained(&mut self, task: TaskId, time: Time);
    
    /// Verify all losers are drained before race returns
    pub fn check(&self) -> Result<(), LoserDrainViolation>;
}
```

## Violation Detection
```rust
pub struct LoserDrainViolation {
    pub race_id: RaceId,
    pub winner: TaskId,
    pub undrained_losers: Vec<(TaskId, DrainRecord)>,
    pub race_return_time: Time,
}
```

A violation occurs when:
1. Race R completes at time T
2. ∃ loser L where `fully_drained` time > T or is None

## Operations That Must Drain Losers
| Operation | Winner | Losers |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| `race(a, b)` | First to complete | Other participants |
| `select\!{...}` | Selected branch | Unselected branches |
| `quorum(M, N)` | First M successes | Remaining N-M |
| `first_ok(...)` | First success | Operations after success |
| `timeout(op, d)` | Whichever wins | Timeout or op |

## Testing the Oracle
1. **Two-way race**: Winner and loser properly handled
2. **N-way race**: Multiple losers all drained
3. **Nested races**: Inner race losers drained before outer race proceeds
4. **Loser with finalizers**: Finalizers run during drain
5. **Loser with obligations**: Obligations resolved during drain
6. **Slow drain**: Loser takes time to reach checkpoint

## Drain Timing Verification
The oracle tracks timing to ensure:
- `race_return_time > max(loser_drain_times)`
- Race does NOT return until all losers fully drained

## References
- asupersync_plan_v4.md: §5.5 Race with Loser Draining, §1.1 Non-negotiable invariants
- asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md: RACE-LOSER-DRAIN rule

## Acceptance Criteria
- Oracle identifies each race and validates that all losers reach terminal completion before the race returns.
- Also validates losers are cancelled (or otherwise transitioned out of running) promptly.
- Produces actionable diagnostics (winner/loser ids, missing completion event).
- Deterministic and runs on every E2E scenario trace.

 {"id":"bd-4nz","title":"[EPIC-TOKIO] Codec Framework (tokio-util codecs equivalent)","description":"# Codec Framework

## Overview
Bidirectional encoding/decoding with framing support.

## Core Traits

### Decoder
```rust
pub trait Decoder {
    type Item;
    type Error;
    fn decode(&mut self, src: &mut BytesMut) -> Result<Option<Self::Item>, Self::Error>;
}
```

### Encoder
```rust
pub trait Encoder<Item> {
    type Error;
    fn encode(&mut self, item: Item, dst: &mut BytesMut) -> Result<(), Self::Error>;
}
```

## Standard Codecs

### 1. LinesCodec
- Newline-delimited text
- Configurable max line length

### 2. LengthDelimitedCodec
- Length-prefixed framing
- Configurable length field (u8, u16, u32, u64)
- Big/little endian

### 3. BytesCodec
- Raw bytes passthrough

### 4. AnyDelimiterCodec
- Custom delimiter support

## Framed Transport

### FramedRead<T, D>
- Combines AsyncRead + Decoder
- Implements Stream

### FramedWrite<T, E>
- Combines AsyncWrite + Encoder
- Implements Sink

### Framed<T, U>
- Full duplex codec transport
- Stream + Sink

## Cancel-Safety
- Decode: partial frame preserved in buffer
- Encode: atomic frame writes
- Framed: tracks partial state

## Bytes Integration
- bytes::Bytes and BytesMut
- Zero-copy where possible

 {"id":"bd-4pl","title":"Implement test oracle: no_task_leaks invariant checker","description":"## Purpose
Implement a test oracle that verifies the "no task leaks" invariant: after any region closes, no tasks spawned within that region remain running. This is one of the 6 non-negotiable invariants.

## The Invariant
From asupersync_plan_v4.md:
> Every spawned task is owned by a region; region close waits for all children

Formally: `∀r ∈ closed_regions: tasks_in_region(r) = ∅`

After `scope.region(...).await` returns, the region's `TaskSet` must be empty (all tasks in Completed state).

## Oracle Design

```rust
pub struct TaskLeakOracle {
    // Tracks all task spawns and completions
    spawns: Vec<(TaskId, RegionId, Time)>,
    completions: Vec<(TaskId, Time)>,
    region_closes: Vec<(RegionId, Time)>,
}

impl TaskLeakOracle {
    /// Called by scheduler on each spawn
    pub fn on_spawn(&mut self, task: TaskId, region: RegionId, time: Time);
    
    /// Called when task reaches Completed
    pub fn on_complete(&mut self, task: TaskId, time: Time);
    
    /// Called when region reaches Closed
    pub fn on_region_close(&mut self, region: RegionId, time: Time);
    
    /// Verify invariant holds
    pub fn check(&self) -> Result<(), TaskLeakViolation>;
}
```

## Violation Detection
```rust
pub struct TaskLeakViolation {
    pub region: RegionId,
    pub leaked_tasks: Vec<TaskId>,
    pub region_close_time: Time,
}
```

A violation occurs when:
1. Region R closes at time T
2. ∃ task spawned in R with no completion record at time ≤ T

## Integration Points
The oracle hooks into:
1. `Cx::spawn()` - records spawn event
2. Task state transition to `Completed` - records completion
3. Region state transition to `Closed` - records close and triggers check

## Lab Runtime Integration
In lab runtime, oracle checks run automatically:
- After each region close
- At end of test execution
- Before schedule replay verification

## Invariant Mapping

| Invariant | Oracle |
|-----------|--------|
| 1. Structured concurrency | **no_task_leaks** |
| 2. Region close = quiescence | quiescence_on_close |
| 3. Cancellation is protocol | (verified by state machine tests) |
| 4. Losers are drained | losers_always_drained |
| 5. No obligation leaks | no_obligation_leaks |
| 6. No ambient authority | no_ambient_authority |

This oracle verifies invariant #1.

## Testing the Oracle Itself
1. **Correct case**: Region with tasks that all complete → check passes
2. **Leak case**: Deliberately break invariant → check catches it
3. **Nested regions**: Parent close requires all descendant tasks done
4. **Cancellation**: Cancelled tasks still count as completed

## Performance Considerations
- Oracle overhead acceptable in lab runtime
- Production runtime: oracle disabled or sampled
- Data structures: consider append-only log vs indexed sets

## References
- asupersync_plan_v4.md: §1.1 Non-negotiable invariants
- asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md: Invariant I1 (task_leak_free)
- AGENTS.md: 6 non-negotiable invariants

## Acceptance Criteria
- Oracle verifies task ownership/leak invariant: every spawned task is eventually completed (no orphans) within a closed region.
- Diagnostics include the set of missing completions and their owning region(s).
- Deterministic and integrated into E2E harness.

 {"id":"bd-4qw","title":"[EPIC-APP] Web Application Framework (axum-like)","description":"# Web Application Framework

## Overview
High-level web framework built on our HTTP and Service layers.

## Routing

### Router
```rust
let app = Router::new()
    .route("/users", get(list_users).post(create_user))
    .route("/users/:id", get(get_user).delete(delete_user))
    .nest("/api", api_routes())
    .layer(TraceLayer::new());
```

### Path Parameters
- Type-safe extraction
- Multiple parameters
- Wildcard routes

### Method Routing
- GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, etc.
- Method chaining

## Extractors

### Common Extractors
- Path<T>: path parameters
- Query<T>: query string
- Json<T>: JSON body
- Form<T>: form data
- State<T>: shared state
- Headers: header map
- TypedHeader<H>: specific header

### Custom Extractors
- FromRequest trait
- FromRequestParts trait

## Responses

### IntoResponse
- Automatic conversion
- Status codes
- Headers
- Body types

### Common Responses
- Json<T>
- Html<T>
- Redirect
- Sse (server-sent events)
- WebSocket upgrade

## Middleware
- Layer-based (tower)
- State injection
- Error handling

## Error Handling
- Custom error types
- Error recovery
- Status code mapping

## State Management
- Shared state with State<T>
- Extension pattern

## WebSockets
- Upgrade handling
- Message framing
- Cancel-safe streaming

## Server-Sent Events
- EventStream type
- Keep-alive
- Retry hints

## Form Handling
- URL-encoded
- Multipart
- File uploads

## Testing
- TestClient for handlers
- Request builders
- Response assertions

 {"id":"bd-4rpn","title":"[Harmonize] Fix priority alignments","description":"# [Harmonize] Fix priority alignments

## Purpose
Ensure priority follows dependency order: parent EPICs have priority ≤ child priorities.

## Issues Fixed
- xrc (Phase 1) was P2, now P1 (blocks ds8 which is P2)
- 90l9 (P0 duplicate) closed/merged into ds8

## Rule
Priority must respect dependencies:
- If A blocks B, then priority(A) ≤ priority(B)
- P0 = Critical, P1 = High, P2 = Medium, P3 = Low, P4 = Backlog

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No priority inversions in dependency graph
- [ ] `bv --robot-priority` shows no misalignments
- [ ] All xrc.* sub-tasks have P1 priority
- [ ] Parent EPICs never have higher priority number than children

## Verification
```bash
bv --robot-priority | jq '.recommendations'  # Should be empty or acceptable
```","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"ClaudeOpus45","created_at":"2026-01-22T18:28:29.875907033Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-23T03:14:34.794684288Z","closed_at":"2026-01-23T02:35:47.483747422Z","close_reason":"Fixed priority inversions:

- 4ul: P1→P2 (blocked by ds8)
- 6ll,9mq: P1→P3 (blocked by P3 items)
- y1p,0vx,ucq,zfn: P1→P2 (cascading fixes)
- tpru: P2→P3, brm: P2→P3, ou7o: P2→P3, 3nm: P2→P3
- z8n,5jm,m76: P1→P2 (blocked by 4ul)
No cycles detected. Core xrc.* tasks are P1, stretch goals (xrc.5,xrc.6) appropriately P3/P4.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-4rpn","depends_on_id":"bd-zs64","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:11Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":124,"issue_id":"bd-4rpn","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Ran a priority inversion scan against .beads/issues.jsonl: 42 inversions where a blocker has lower priority (numerically higher).  {"id":"bd-akx.8.3","title":"Implement map_reduce combinator (monoid-based aggregation)","description":"# map_reduce (Monoid-Based Parallel Aggregation)

## Purpose
Provide a derived combinator that expresses parallel map followed by reduction under an associative operation (monoid). This is called out explicitly as a derived combinator in the design.

## Semantics
- Spawn N tasks that each compute a partial result.
- Join all.
- Reduce results using an associative combine function.

## Requirements
- Must preserve structured concurrency: no detached work.
- Cancellation:
  - If the parent region cancels, all children cancel/drain.
  - If a child errors and policy is fail-fast, siblings cancel/drain.
- Deterministic lab runtime behavior (for a fixed schedule/seed).

## API Sketch
```rust
pub async fn map_reduce<I, F, T>(
    scope: &Scope<'_>,
    inputs: I,
    map: impl Fn(I::Item) -> F,
    reduce: impl Fn(T, T) -> T,
) -> Outcome<T>
where
    I: IntoIterator,
    F: Future<Output = Outcome<T>>,
{
    // spawn map futures
    // join_all
    // reduce
}
```

## Notes
- Reduction order may affect determinism if `reduce` is not commutative; we must define the reduction order (e.g., input order) and document it.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All tasks complete or are cancelled/drained before return.
- Reduction order is documented and deterministic.

## Testing
- Unit test with associative reduce.
- Negative test demonstrating non-associative reduce yields schedule-dependent results (documented).

 {"id":"bd-akx.9","title":"Phase 0: Verification (Oracles, Unit Tests, E2E, Benches)","description":"# Phase 0: Verification (Oracles, Unit Tests, E2E, Benches)

## Purpose
Asupersync's guarantees are only real if we can continuously *prove them operationally*.

This feature organizes the verification surface:
- invariants (tree structure, task ownership, quiescence, no leaks, loser draining, cancel propagation, no ambient authority, determinism)
- unit tests
- E2E scenario tests
- baseline benchmarks

## Required Oracles (Phase 0)
The spec's invariants from asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md §5 require these trace- or state-checkable oracles:

| Invariant | Oracle |
|-----------|--------|
| INV-TREE | region_tree_valid |
| INV-TASK-OWNED | no_task_leaks |
| INV-QUIESCENCE | quiescence_on_close |
| INV-CANCEL-PROPAGATES | cancellation_protocol_valid |
| INV-OBLIGATION-BOUNDED | no_obligation_leaks |
| INV-OBLIGATION-LINEAR | no_obligation_leaks |
| INV-MASK-BOUNDED | cancellation_protocol_valid |
| INV-DEADLINE-MONOTONE | deadline_monotone |
| INV-LOSER-DRAINED | losers_always_drained |

Additionally:
- all_finalizers_ran: Verify LIFO finalizer execution
- no_ambient_authority: Verify effects only via Cx
- determinism: same seed/config => identical trace

## Algebraic Laws Testing
The algebraic laws from asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md §7 require property-based tests:
- LAW-JOIN-ASSOC
- LAW-JOIN-COMM
- LAW-RACE-COMM
- LAW-TIMEOUT-MIN
- LAW-RACE-NEVER
- LAW-RACE-JOIN-DIST

## Acceptance Criteria
- `cargo test` covers the invariants above with deterministic lab runtime.
- E2E scenarios exist for:
  - nested regions
  - cancellation end-to-end
  - race draining
  - two-phase channels under cancellation
  - replay/determinism
- Benchmark suite exists to set Phase 0 baselines (spawn cost, cancel path, channel ops) without regressing determinism.

## Logging & Debuggability
- Tests must emit detailed, structured diagnostics on failure:
  - dump formatted trace
  - show first divergence step for replay
  - show invariant violation evidence

 {"id":"bd-akx.9.1","title":"Implement determinism oracle: identical seed/config => identical trace","description":"# Determinism Oracle (Seed/Config => Identical Trace)

## Purpose
One of the non-negotiable invariants is **Determinism is first-class**. In Phase 0, this means:

> Given the same lab configuration (including seed) and the same user program, the runtime produces the same observable trace.

This oracle makes that guarantee executable.

## What This Oracle Checks
For a chosen scenario/program `P` and lab config `C`:
1. Run `P` under `C` and capture trace `T1`.
2. Run `P` again under the *same* `C` and capture trace `T2`.
3. Assert `T1 == T2` (byte-for-byte or structurally equal).

If not equal, report:
- first divergence index
- expected event vs actual event
- surrounding context window
- relevant runtime snapshot (optional)

## Design Notes
- Determinism must include:
  - task selection decisions
  - timer wake ordering
  - cancellation propagation ordering
  - obligation IDs (or stable renaming normalization)

### ID renaming normalization (important)
If IDs are allocated in a deterministic order, raw equality is fine.
If not, we must canonicalize traces by renaming “fresh IDs” consistently before comparison.

Phase 0 goal: **make ID allocation deterministic** so canonicalization is minimal.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The oracle exists as a helper (e.g., `LabRuntime::assert_deterministic(program)` or standalone function).
- At least 3 E2E scenarios use it:
  - nested regions
  - race + loser draining
  - two-phase channel under cancellation

## Testing
- Intentionally break determinism (e.g., by using wall-clock time) in a test-only “bad runtime” to ensure oracle detects divergence.

 {"id":"bd-anz","title":"[Foundation] Symbol Authentication and Security","description":"# Symbol Authentication and Security

## Overview
Provides authentication primitives for the RaptorQ-based distributed layer, enabling verification of symbol integrity and authenticity during transmission across untrusted networks.

## Implementation Status
**Partially Complete** - Core types implemented, integration and tests pending.

## Design Principles

1. **Determinism-compatible**: All operations are deterministic for lab runtime
2. **Interface-first**: Clean traits allow swapping implementations
3. **No ambient keys**: Keys must be explicitly provided (capability security)
4. **Fail-safe defaults**: Invalid/missing auth fails closed

## Architecture

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    SecurityContext                        │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │                      AuthKey                        │ │
│  │  • 256-bit key material                            │ │
│  │  • Deterministic derivation from seed/DetRng       │ │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                          │                               │
│                          ▼                               │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │                    Authenticator                    │ │
│  │  • sign(symbol) → AuthenticationTag                │ │
│  │  • verify(symbol, tag) → Result<(), AuthError>     │ │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
│                          │                               │
│                          ▼                               │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
│  │               AuthenticatedSymbol                   │ │
│  │  • Symbol + AuthenticationTag bundle               │ │
│  │  • Verified on construction, unverified on receive │ │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Core Types (Implemented)

### AuthKey
```rust
pub struct AuthKey {
    bytes: [u8; AUTH_KEY_SIZE], // 32 bytes
}

impl AuthKey {
    pub fn from_seed(seed: u64) -> Self;
    pub fn from_rng(rng: &mut DetRng) -> Self;
    pub fn from_bytes(bytes: [u8; AUTH_KEY_SIZE]) -> Self;
    pub fn derive_subkey(&self, purpose: &[u8]) -> Self;
    pub fn as_bytes(&self) -> &[u8; AUTH_KEY_SIZE];
}
```

### AuthenticationTag
```rust
pub struct AuthenticationTag {
    bytes: [u8; TAG_SIZE], // 32 bytes
}

impl AuthenticationTag {
    pub fn compute(key: &AuthKey, symbol: &Symbol) -> Self;
    pub fn verify(&self, key: &AuthKey, symbol: &Symbol) -> bool;
    pub fn zero() -> Self;
    pub fn from_bytes(bytes: [u8; TAG_SIZE]) -> Self;
    pub fn as_bytes(&self) -> &[u8; TAG_SIZE];
}
```

### SecurityContext
```rust
pub struct SecurityContext {
    key: AuthKey,
    mode: AuthMode,
    stats: AuthStats,
}

pub enum AuthMode {
    Strict,      // Verification failures are errors
    Permissive,  // Failures logged but allowed
    Disabled,    // Skip verification entirely
}

impl SecurityContext {
    pub fn new(key: AuthKey) -> Self;
    pub fn for_testing(seed: u64) -> Self;
    pub fn with_mode(self, mode: AuthMode) -> Self;
    pub fn sign_symbol(&mut self, symbol: &Symbol) -> AuthenticatedSymbol;
    pub fn verify_authenticated_symbol(&mut self, auth: &AuthenticatedSymbol) -> Result<(), AuthError>;
    pub fn derive_context(&self, purpose: &[u8]) -> Self;
    pub fn stats(&self) -> &AuthStats;
}
```

### AuthenticatedSymbol
```rust
pub struct AuthenticatedSymbol {
    symbol: Symbol,
    tag: AuthenticationTag,
    verified: bool,
}

impl AuthenticatedSymbol {
    pub fn new_verified(symbol: Symbol, tag: AuthenticationTag) -> Self;
    pub fn from_parts(symbol: Symbol, tag: AuthenticationTag) -> Self;
    pub fn symbol(&self) -> &Symbol;
    pub fn tag(&self) -> &AuthenticationTag;
    pub fn is_verified(&self) -> bool;
    pub fn into_symbol(self) -> Symbol;
}
```

## Remaining Work

### 1. Integration with Transport Layer
- [ ] Automatic signing in SymbolSink implementations
- [ ] Automatic verification in SymbolStream implementations
- [ ] Key negotiation for multi-party transport

### 2. Key Rotation Support
- [ ] Key versioning in AuthenticationTag
- [ ] Graceful key rotation protocol
- [ ] Old key acceptance window

### 3. Performance Optimization
- [ ] Batch verification (amortize overhead)
- [ ] Parallel tag computation
- [ ] Cache for repeated verifications

## Phase 0 Note

The current implementation uses a deterministic keyed hash that is NOT cryptographically secure. Production deployments MUST use a proper HMAC implementation (e.g., HMAC-SHA256).

## Unit Test Requirements

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    // Key generation
    #[test] fn test_from_seed_deterministic() {}
    #[test] fn test_from_seed_different_seeds() {}
    #[test] fn test_from_rng_produces_unique_keys() {}
    #[test] fn test_from_bytes_roundtrip() {}

    // Key derivation
    #[test] fn test_derive_subkey_deterministic() {}
    #[test] fn test_derive_subkey_different_purposes() {}
    #[test] fn test_derived_key_not_equal_to_master() {}

    // Tag computation
    #[test] fn test_compute_deterministic() {}
    #[test] fn test_verify_valid_tag() {}
    #[test] fn test_verify_fails_different_data() {}
    #[test] fn test_verify_fails_different_key() {}

    // SecurityContext
    #[test] fn test_sign_and_verify() {}
    #[test] fn test_strict_mode_fails_bad_tag() {}
    #[test] fn test_permissive_mode_allows_failures() {}
    #[test] fn test_disabled_mode_skips_verification() {}

    // Security properties
    #[test] fn test_debug_does_not_leak_key_material() {}
    #[test] fn test_zero_tag_fails_verification() {}
}
```

## Logging Strategy

```rust
tracing::debug!(mode = ?ctx.mode, "SecurityContext created");
tracing::trace!(symbol_id = %id, "Symbol signed");
tracing::trace!(symbol_id = %id, verified = result.is_ok(), "Symbol verification");
tracing::warn!(symbol_id = %id, "Authentication failed in permissive mode");
tracing::error!(symbol_id = %id, "Authentication failed in strict mode");
```

## Dependencies
- Depends on: asupersync-p80 (Symbol types) [CLOSED]
- Blocks: asupersync-eg7 (Security tests), asupersync-hq6 (SymbolStream/Sink), asupersync-2m2 (Aggregator), asupersync-86i (Router)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [x] AuthKey generation and derivation
- [x] AuthenticationTag computation and verification
- [x] SecurityContext with mode selection
- [x] AuthenticatedSymbol wrapper type
- [ ] Transport layer integration
- [ ] Comprehensive test coverage
- [ ] Performance benchmarks","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CloudyOpus","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-17T08:52:48.541287610Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-17T21:24:05.725986221Z","closed_at":"2026-01-17T21:24:05.725986221Z","close_reason":"Implemented core security types, context, and tests","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-anz","depends_on_id":"bd-p80","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:01:54Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"bd-aqn","title":"Implement hedge combinator for latency hedging","description":"## Purpose
The hedge combinator implements latency hedging - start a primary task, and if it does not complete within a deadline, speculatively start a backup. Return whichever completes first. This is a key pattern for reducing tail latency in distributed systems.

## Motivation
P99 latencies often exceed P50 by 10-100x. Hedging trades compute cost for latency:
- Primary starts immediately
- If deadline expires without completion, backup launches
- First to complete wins; loser is cancelled and drained
- Total latency bounded by min(primary, backup) rather than max

## Semantic Model

```rust
pub async fn hedge<T, E>(
    cx: &mut Cx<'_>,
    primary: impl Future<Output = Result<T, E>>,
    backup: impl FnOnce(&mut Cx<'_>) -> impl Future<Output = Result<T, E>>,
    deadline: Duration,
) -> Outcome<T, E>
```

### Behavior
1. Spawn primary as region child
2. Start timer for deadline
3. Case A - Primary completes before deadline: return result, never spawn backup
4. Case B - Deadline fires: spawn backup as region child, race primary vs backup
5. Loser of race MUST be cancelled and drained (non-negotiable)
6. Return winner's result

### Budget Semantics
From the spec: hedge operations have combined budget = primary_budget + backup_budget + deadline
The deadline acts as a "grace period" before hedging kicks in.

## Cancellation Handling
- If caller requests cancel before primary completes: cancel primary, never spawn backup
- If caller requests cancel during race: cancel both, drain both
- Loser draining is mandatory regardless of outcome

## Implementation Notes
- Backup is a `FnOnce` closure, not a future - only create backup future if needed
- This avoids allocating/preparing backup work that may never execute
- The closure takes `Cx` to spawn into the same region

## Invariant Support
- **Losers always drained**: If backup spawned, exactly one loses and must drain
- **No orphan tasks**: Both primary and backup owned by hedge region
- **Quiescence**: Hedge region closes only when all spawned children done

## Testing Requirements
1. Primary fast path (completes before deadline)
2. Backup triggered path (deadline expires)
3. Both cases: winner returns, loser drained
4. Cancellation at each phase
5. Budget propagation verification
6. Deterministic lab runtime testing

## Example Usage

```rust
// Primary RPC with 100ms hedge to backup
let result = scope.hedge(
    cx,
    call_primary_server(cx, request.clone()),
    |cx| call_backup_server(cx, request.clone()),
    Duration::from_millis(100),
).await?;
```

## Real-World Applications
- Database reads with replica fallback
- RPC calls with backup endpoint
- DNS resolution with multiple resolvers
- Storage operations with tiered backends

## References
- asupersync_plan_v4.md: §5.7 Derived Combinators
- Google "The Tail at Scale" paper (Dean & Barroso)
- asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md: §3.2 Budget composition

## Acceptance Criteria
- Starts a secondary attempt after a deterministic delay (virtual time in lab) to reduce tail latency.
- Ensures only one winner is committed; losers are cancelled and drained.
- Uses cancel-safe primitives for any shared result publication.
- E2E tests cover determinism, cancellation, and no-obligation-leaks.

 {"id":"bd-aqy","title":"[EPIC-TOKIO] Async Filesystem (tokio-fs equivalent)","description":"# Async Filesystem Operations

## Overview
Async file I/O with proper cancel-safety and obligation tracking.

## Components

### 1. File Operations
- open, create, read, write, flush, sync
- seek, truncate
- metadata, set_permissions

### 2. Directory Operations
- create_dir, create_dir_all
- read_dir (async iterator)
- remove_dir, remove_dir_all

### 3. Path Operations
- canonicalize
- rename, copy
- remove_file
- symlink, hard_link

### 4. Buffered I/O
- BufReader, BufWriter for files
- Efficient buffering with cancel-safety

## Cancel-Safety Strategy
- Reads: Cancel safe (discard partial)
- Writes: Two-phase (reserve space, commit)
- Directory iteration: Cancel at any point

## Platform Abstraction
- Linux: io_uring for true async
- Other: Thread pool for blocking ops

## Lab Runtime
- Virtual filesystem
- Deterministic ordering
- Fault injection (disk full, permission denied)

## Integration
- Works with AsyncRead/AsyncWrite traits
- Integrates with codec layer
